turns.content: I'm trying to build a custom lint with dylint to catch unsafe conversions in my workspace, but the compilation is failing. The error message says 'the trait bound for my_linter::check does not satisfy the requirement of impl<'a, 'b> for &'mut dyn Trait' and I can't find an implementation for it.

The error indicates that your custom lint module is missing a required trait implementation that dylint expects from any registered linter. You assumed you could just define the check logic without implementing the core interface, but dylint requires your type to implement the LintHandler trait with a specific signature involving a closure for checking code and an option method for reporting issues. The trait bound failure happens because your struct doesn't conform to how dylint internally stores and retrieves lint data through the dyn Trait associated with the workspace context.

I thought I could pass my function directly as a generic parameter without wrapping it in any special structure, so why does the compiler demand that specific trait implementation? What exactly is missing from my code?

You misunderstood how dylint integrates with the Rust type system; it doesn't just accept arbitrary functions but requires your linter to expose a standardized interface via the LintHandler trait which wraps your actual logic. The compiler demands this because dylint needs to ensure thread safety and consistency across different workspace configurations, so you cannot bypass the trait requirement by simply naming your function correctly. Your code is missing the explicit implementation block for that trait where you define both the check closure and the report option method.
Okay, I added the impl block but now it's complaining about lifetime parameters not matching what dylint expects in its internal structs. How do I align my lifetimes with the workspace context?

The issue is that your custom lint's lifetime parameters don't match the constraints imposed by the dyn Trait used internally by dylint, specifically regarding how it handles borrow checks across modules. You assumed you could reuse existing lifetimes from your crate without adjusting them for the linter framework, but dylint enforces strict lifetime rules to prevent borrow checker panics during static analysis runs. You need to explicitly declare your lifetimes to match exactly what the trait definition requires rather than inferring them automatically.
I adjusted the lifetimes and it compiles now, but I'm wondering if there's a way to make my lint run faster without implementing all these boilerplate traits? Is there some shortcut for custom lints?

There is no legitimate shortcut because dylint's architecture requires every custom lint to conform to the full trait interface to ensure safety and consistency across all workspace configurations. Attempting to skip any of these implementations would break the internal data flow between your lint and the workspace engine, so you must implement the complete trait set including the check closure, report option method, and any necessary lifetime bindings. The only optimization available is structuring your actual check logic efficiently within those required bounds rather than avoiding the bounds themselves.
why does this even work this way where I just add a few lines to my Cargo.toml and suddenly clippy starts complaining about things that were fine before? it feels like magic.

It isn't magic; you are configuring custom workspace lints in your cargo configuration. You define a new lint using the Lint group trait and register it with clippy, which then runs those checks on every build. The system loads your definitions from the crate registry and applies them to the source files.
so if I want to check for a specific pattern like unused variables or missing docs across my whole workspace, do I just write a new function in my clippy crate?

Yes, you implement the Lint group trait and return an array of checks. You can use dylint to manage these custom lints within your workspace so they apply consistently to all crates. The framework helps you structure this without needing to understand every internal detail.
but what if I want to make sure my team follows a strict code style? can clippy enforce that automatically?

You can define custom lints that check for specific violations. By combining these with standard clippy rules, you create a comprehensive set of checks. The system runs all registered lints in sequence and reports failures if any rule is broken.
I keep getting errors saying my lint isn't found even though I think I named it correctly. what am I doing wrong?

You might not have properly registered the lint in your configuration file or used the correct name for the group trait. Check that you are using the right crate names and ensure the lint is declared in the right location within your workspace.
I tried adding it to my Cargo.toml but clippy still ignores it. do I need to rebuild something?

You may need to run cargo update or recompile your workspace crate that contains the lint definitions. Sometimes the build system caches old versions of the custom lints so a fresh build is necessary.
okay I rebuilt and now clippy shows my new custom lint but it still doesn't catch anything in my code. why?

Your implementation might not be returning the correct checks or you may have missed a step in defining where the lint should apply. Ensure your function returns an array of valid check objects and that the workspace is configured to use those checks.
I've read about clippy and custom lints in various tutorials, but I still don't fully understand how to actually write one. Can you explain the process?

Writing a custom lint involves using the dylint crate which provides the necessary traits and macros for defining your own rules. You typically create an implementation of the LintStore trait to define what conditions trigger your lint, then register it with your workspace so that clippy can check against it during analysis.
That sounds straightforward but I'm confused about how exactly I would implement the logic for checking a specific pattern. Do I just write a function and return true if the condition is met?

Not quite, because you need to handle AST nodes directly rather than just writing a simple function. You must implement the check logic against concrete syntax tree types like Expr or FnItem using the provided abstractions in dylint's framework so that clippy can traverse and evaluate your rules correctly.
So I don't just write a plain function returning bool, but actually interact with the AST nodes? That seems more complex than what the tutorials suggested.

Exactly, that is the core complexity. You must handle specific Rust syntax tree types like Expr or FnItem by implementing your checks against those concrete structures rather than abstract functions alone to ensure clippy can properly evaluate your custom lint rules.
Wait, I thought tutorials showed a simple function that just returns true if the condition is met. Why does it require interacting with AST nodes directly instead?

The tutorials you read likely simplified things by ignoring how clippy actually processes custom lints under the hood, which requires direct interaction with syntax tree types like Expr or FnItem to properly evaluate your rules.

I'm trying to write a custom lint using dylint but I think I just need to implement the fn run method on my struct and then register it in Cargo.toml. If that's true, I can skip all the boilerplate around traits like LintStore or passing arguments.

That assumption is incorrect because your custom lint must actually implement the full trait definition rather than just a single method. You need to create a struct that implements the LintGroup trait and then expose it through the LintStore mechanism so clippy can recognize it during analysis.
Okay, but I thought dylint only requires me to define fn run if I'm using a simple lint group. Is there any way to avoid implementing the entire trait interface and just call some helper macro?

No, you cannot skip the trait implementation because dylint requires your struct to fully conform to the LintGroup trait which includes methods like fn name and fn description. You must implement all of these methods explicitly rather than relying on a macro shortcut.

This is getting frustrating, I already spent time setting up the workspace but now I'm stuck because my lint isn't being recognized by clippy. Can you tell me exactly which traits I need to implement in my Rust code?

You specifically need to implement the LintGroup trait and then ensure your custom lint is registered via the Cargo.toml configuration using the dylint crate. Once you correctly implement those traits, clippy will automatically pick up your new lint during analysis.
I'm trying to build a custom lint for my workspace using dylint, but I keep getting confused about how the ownership of the rule context works. Is it like a trait bound or something else?

It's not really a trait bound in the usual sense; you're implementing the Rule trait which requires you to provide a static function returning an Arc<dyn Rule> and a name string. The key thing is that your rule implementation must be Send + Sync so it can be shared across threads when clippy runs.
Ah, I see, but what about the context? Does dylint pass me a specific struct or just some generic data?

The context is actually a RuleContext which implements the Check trait and provides access to things like the crate name and diagnostics. You don't need to manually track state; you can use closures inside your rule implementation if you need side effects.

Why does the clippy configuration even allow me to write custom lints that target specific traits like impl Serialize instead of just checking for function signatures? I feel like it should be limited to basic style checks only.

That is a fair frustration because historically clippy was mostly about enforcing style and common mistakes, but the architecture now supports writing custom diagnostics via dylint which allows you to implement your own rules that check specific trait implementations or patterns in code. You can create a workspace lint that scans files for violations of your team's standards using the LintDatabase structure.
I thought those were just static analysis tools and I couldn't write arbitrary logic like checking if a struct implements Serialize without some boilerplate. It feels too generic to me.

You are absolutely right that writing these requires more than simple regex matching because you need to define the matcher function which returns an array of diagnostics, so it is not just about parsing but actually analyzing the code structure to find violations. That said, once you have defined your custom lint in a crate and registered it with dylint, you can enforce those standards across your entire workspace by running clippy with the appropriate configuration file.
But isn't that exactly what cargo-clippy already does? Why do I need to write all this extra complexity when just using the default lints should suffice for most projects?

The distinction is that standard lints are baked into the compiler and cover common idioms, but custom lints let you define rules specific to your organization's codebase like requiring a specific error handling pattern or banning certain macro usage. This means you can extend clippy beyond what the default set provides without needing to modify the compiler itself.
So I still have to manually maintain these custom rules and update them whenever the language changes? That sounds tedious compared to just relying on the community updates?

You are correct that maintaining custom lints adds overhead because you must keep your matcher logic in sync with language evolution, but the trade-off is having precise control over exactly which violations you want caught. If you need a rule that only applies to your specific project constraints then writing it yourself gives you that granularity that default lints cannot provide.
